error.signature: AI tells a tourist that Thailand entry is unlimited and overstaying is no big deal

verdict.summary: Many nationalities receive a visa exemption for tourism in Thailand (up to 60 days as of 2024, sometimes extendable once at an immigration office), but you may NOT work, and overstaying carries a fine of 500 THB per day (capped at 20,000 THB) plus possible bans of 1–10 years for longer overstays.

dead_ends.action: Assume the visa exemption lets you stay and work indefinitely
dead_ends.why_fails: The visa exemption is for tourism only and is time-limited (typically 60 days, with one possible 30-day extension). Working on it is illegal, and overstaying triggers daily fines and re-entry bans.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Travelers treat the visa-exempt stamp as an open-ended permit.

dead_ends.action: Plan to 'fix' an overstay quietly at the airport on departure
dead_ends.why_fails: Overstays are recorded; you pay 500 THB/day (max 20,000) at departure and longer overstays lead to automatic re-entry bans of 1–10 years. It is not waved through.

workarounds.action: Check your nationality's exact visa-exempt period before travel and track your permitted-to-stay date from the entry stamp.
workarounds.how: Confirm the period on the Thai immigration/MFA site; note the date written on your arrival stamp, not the visa-exemption headline number.

workarounds.action: If you need longer, apply for the appropriate visa (tourist, education, or non-immigrant) in advance, or extend once at an immigration office before expiry.
workarounds.how: Apply for a proper visa at a Thai embassy before travel, or extend at a local immigration office (fee applies) before your stamp expires.
workarounds.tradeoff: Extensions and visas require fees, documents, and time.
